subject: Mathematics
title: Circular Reasoning
description: A short lesson to help you apply Circular Reasoning.

hook.text: 'This book is true because it says so inside.' That argument goes in a circle.

buildup.text: Circular reasoning uses the conclusion as a premise. It assumes what it's trying to prove.

discovery.text: It often hides behind complex wording: 'Freedom is important because liberty matters most.'

twist.text: Circular arguments are technically valid—the conclusion does follow from the premises. But they prove nothing new.

climax.text: Breaking the circle requires introducing independent evidence that doesn't assume the conclusion.

punchline.text: If your proof is your claim, you've proven nothing.

quiz.question: What is wrong with circular reasoning?
options: The conclusion is assumed in the premises
